AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of sending), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had sent them' entails a resulting change of state (the crowd was dispatched as a consequence) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a change-of-state event (the act of sending) involving dynamic progression and a clear endpoint, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the sending occurred before the approaching), not to mark a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative world.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had sent' entails a change of possession/agency and a resulting state (the crowd has been dispatched and is now approaching), which is relevant to the narrative progression and holds at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had sent' describes a change of possession/delegation (the chief priests and elders caused the crowd to be sent), and the resulting state—that the crowd has been sent and is now approaching—persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' describes a discrete bounded event (the sending action) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had sent' presents the sending as a single bounded, completed event that occurred prior to the narrative sequence, construing the action as a discrete whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had sent' is a past perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (the chief priests and elders had previously sent the crowd); it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had sent' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'The chief priest and elders' as the semantic agent (doers) and 'them' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle'.

FullText: » This has taken place so that the writings of the prophets will come true . « Then all his disciples left him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an idiom functioning as an intransitive verb with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'place' is part of the fixed expression and does not function as a direct object receiving the action.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' licenses an external argument (the event itself as agent/initiator of the occurrence), and the construction denotes an event that happens rather than a change-of-state or motion of the sole argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' denotes an event of existence or occurrence without projecting an external argument (agent/initiator); the subject 'this' functions as a theme/patient undergoing the event rather than as an agent initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the occurrence/happening of an event) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (an event occurring at a definable temporal boundary) and naturally accepts time-point adverbials, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' denotes a completed, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the occurrence of an event), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has taken place' entails a transition into a resulting state (the prophets' writings coming true as a consequence) and is construed as a completed event with a resulting condition, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an event with dynamic change and an internal boundary (occurrence of an event), which is incompatible with stative readings; the present perfect 'has taken place' marks completion of this bounded eventive situation.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' denotes a bounded event whose resulting state (that the event occurred) is relevant for the subsequent discourse explaining why the prophets' writings will come true, making it narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an event verb describing an occurrence without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause reports that an event happened in the past, not that a changed state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (something that has taken place/occurred) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual series indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'taken place' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized event in the actual world, with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'taken', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has taken place' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); instead it is a perfect aspect construction with the active verb 'taken', where the subject 'This' is the semantic agent performing the event.

FullText: Then the high priest ripped his robes apart and said : » He has said evil against God ! Why do we need more witnesses ? You have heard his words of blasphemy ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words of blasphemy'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase; hearing is construed as an instantaneous boundary event of coming into auditory perception, not a gradual process culminating in a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of the blasphemous words, occurring at a definable temporal boundary without measurable internal duration, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of his words of blasphemy, which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/perception (having acquired the information of hearing) and does not construe as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing/perceiving the words), which involves eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the narrative world (the witnesses did hear the blasphemous words), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' is a perception verb focusing on the occurrence of an experience (hearing the blasphemous words) as part of the addressees' life history, rather than on a resulting state, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' describes a perception event (hearing/listening) that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past interaction without a persisting condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of hearing specific words) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' presents hearing as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, functioning as a discrete step in the narrative sequence where the high priest appeals to the witnesses' completed perception of the blasphemous words.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is in the second person present perfect ('You have heard'), asserting a realized fact in the actual world—the listeners have actually heard the blasphemous words—with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'heard' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent (the one who heard), and the grammatical object 'his words of blasphemy' as the semantic patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle).

FullText: He said : » I have done wrong in giving a righteous man to you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' in 'have done wrong' functions as a light verb with 'wrong' as a predicative complement rather than introducing an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), making it non-transitive in the ASTrns sense.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' (with 'wrong' as its object) licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('wrong'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' licenses an internal argument ('in giving a righteous man to you'), which is a prepositional phrase specifying the content/manner of the wrongdoing, thus failing the criterion for ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' denotes a punctual moral transgression or boundary violation without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it is construed as a single instantaneous act rather than a gradual change culminating in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have done wrong' denotes a durative or habitual action (wrongdoing as an extended state or repeated behavior) rather than a punctual transition into a new result state, and it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the way achievements require.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have done wrong' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the wrongdoing is finished), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have done wrong' entails a resulting change of state (the wrongdoing has occurred and created a consequent condition of having acted wrongly), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' denotes a completed action with dynamic change and an event boundary (the act of wrongdoing), which involves eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('I have done wrong'), without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have done wrong' functions as a claim about a wrongful action in the subject's history, focusing on the occurrence of the event itself rather than a resulting state, which aligns with the experiential interpretation.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' describes an action/activity rather than a change of state with a resulting condition that persists at reference time; the clause reports a past wrongdoing without entailing a current resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the act of giving a righteous man) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual series present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' presents a transition event (doing wrong) as a single bounded, completed occurrence, construed as a whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' is embedded in a direct speech assertion ('I have done wrong') that reports a realized past action as fact, not as projected, hypothetical, or desired; no modal auxiliary, conditional, or irrealis marker is present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'done', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have done wrong' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); instead, it is active voice with 'have' as a perfect auxiliary and 'done' as the past participle of the transitive verb 'do', where the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action.

FullText: While he was seated in judgment his wife sent a message to him . She said , » Do not judge that righteous man . I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have had' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('much trouble'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object 'much trouble', making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have had' licenses an internal argument ('much trouble'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have had trouble' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous entry into a state of distress (a boundary transition into an experiential state) rather than a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint, and lacks the inherent goal-directedness or measurable culmination required for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have had much trouble' unfolds over a measurable duration (the entire day) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it naturally permits the durative reading of an extended state or experience.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have had much trouble' denotes a bounded, completed experiential state with an inherent endpoint (the trouble occurred and is now in the past), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have had much trouble' entails a resulting state of distress/suffering that persists from the dream experience, and unfolds over a measurable duration ('this day'), thus failing the punctual, non-resultative criteria for semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have had much trouble' denotes a change-of-state and dynamic experience (trouble/suffering) that occurred during a specific time period ('this day in a dream'), involving internal eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him' presents a situation that actually occurred (the wife experienced trouble in a dream), without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not happen.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('this day'), indicating a continuing situation of trouble experienced during a specific time period rather than a completed life experience entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have had trouble' describes an experiential state (suffering/enduring trouble during a dream) rather than a change of state with a resulting condition that persists at reference time; the duration adverbial 'this day' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a new state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event ('trouble in a dream') that occurred during a specific time period ('this day'), not a continuous situation holding from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him' presents a bounded experiential event completed within a defined temporal frame ('this day'), construed as a single whole occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have had much trouble' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the wife's experienced suffering during the dream), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have had' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'had', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have had much trouble' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); 'trouble' is a noun object, not a verbal past participle, and the subject 'I' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: » What evil has he done ? « Pilate asked . But they shouted : » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' introduces an overt internal argument 'evil' (direct object), satisfying the criterion for a two-participant transitive event with external argument (he) and internal argument (evil).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (he/agent) and introduces a direct object (evil), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('evil'), which functions as a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have done evil' denotes a durative process of committing harmful acts that culminates in a defined result state (evil having been committed), and can be naturally modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'what evil has he done in three years?'), satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done evil' denotes a punctual transition into a state of having committed wrongdoing, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'What evil has he done today?'), and represents a boundary event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have done evil' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (the evil deed is finished), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have done evil' entails a resulting change of state (evil has been committed and exists as a completed action with consequences), not a single punctual occurrence without result, so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'done' denotes a completed action or change-of-state event (evil acts performed), which involves dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (asking about real past actions), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The Perfect 'has he done evil' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once, without asserting a resulting state—a perception/communication verb focusing on occurrence rather than result.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do' with the object 'evil' describes a change of state (committing wrongdoing) where the resulting state of having committed evil persists at the reference time, meeting the criterion for resultative aspect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' describes discrete bounded events (specific evil acts) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has done evil' presents a completed action as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, instantiating the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done evil' appears in the interrogative clause 'What evil has he done?' which asserts a realized past action (perfect aspect) as a factual question about actual events, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'done', and 'evil' is the grammatical object corresponding to the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice with the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology.

FullText: About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , » Eli , Eli , lama sabachthani ? « Which translated means : » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and takes a direct object ('me'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct grammatical object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a punctual state-change (transition into the condition of being abandoned) without an inherent durative process phase leading to completion, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of abandonment, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have you forsaken me at this moment'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a completed state change with an inherent endpoint (the state of being abandoned), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have forsaken' entails a resulting change of state (transition from being with/supporting to abandoning), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a change-of-state (abandonment/separation) involving dynamic eventive unfolding from a prior state of presence to a state of absence, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a direct utterance expressing an actual emotional state or complaint in the present moment, not a counterfactual condition; it describes a situation understood to have occurred (being forsaken), not one contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' entails a change of state (abandonment/withdrawal of divine presence) whose resulting state is relevant to the narrative context and Jesus's current suffering, making it non-experiential rather than a simple life history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' describes a change of state (abandonment/withdrawal of support) where the resulting state of being abandoned persists at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of forsaking/abandonment) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' presents the act of forsaking as a completed transition event (a change from being with to being abandoned), construed as a bounded whole rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' appears in a direct speech quotation asserting a realized state of affairs (Jesus's actual experience of abandonment), not a projected, hypothetical, or conditional situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have you forsaken' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'me' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: He laid it in his own new tomb , which he had cut out of the rock . Then he rolled a great stone in front of the door of the tomb , and departed .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'cut' introduces an overt external argument (he) and an overt internal argument (it/the tomb out of the rock), satisfying the transitive pattern with both subject and direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an external argument (agent 'he') and introduces a direct object ('it'/'tomb'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an internal argument (the rock, realized as 'out of the rock'), making it a transitive verb with both external argument (he) and internal argument (the rock/tomb), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'cut out of the rock' involves a durative process of carving/removing material directed toward a defined goal (a completed tomb), naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'cut out in weeks'), and culminates in a result state (a finished tomb structure).
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'cut out' denotes a durative process of carving stone that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it naturally permits the complement 'finish' (e.g., 'finish cutting out the tomb').
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'had cut out of' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (a finished tomb carved from rock), naturally modified by 'in X time' rather than 'for X time', and lacks the homogeneous, durative quality required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had cut out of the rock' entails a resulting change of state (a tomb carved from rock), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it describes a completed action with a persistent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'cut out' denotes a dynamic change-of-state action involving internal eventive unfolding and progression (carving stone from rock), which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'which he had cut out of the rock' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the cutting occurred before the laying), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative world.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'cut out of' entails a change of state (creating a tomb from rock), and that resulting state—the existence of the carved tomb—holds at the reference time and is relevant for the subsequent narrative action of rolling the stone in front of it.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'cut out' describes a change of state (rock transformed from uncut to carved), and the resulting state (a tomb existing in the rock) persists at the reference time when he laid the body in it.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'which he had cut out of the rock' describes a discrete, bounded past action (the cutting of the tomb) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had cut out of' presents the action of cutting as a completed, bounded transition event that occurred prior to the main narrative events, construing the situation as a single whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had cut out of' is a past perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (the tomb that he had previously cut), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, forming a past perfect construction ('had cut'), which instantiates TLTS according to the rule 'The auxiliary is morphologically past.'
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had cut out of' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one performing the cutting action), not the patient.

FullText: The angel spoke to the women : » I know you seek Jesus who has been impaled ; do not fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been impaled'), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, realized as the relative pronoun 'who' referring to Jesus), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (has been impaled), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who performed the impaling), which disqualifies it from ASUncc status.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'impaled' licenses an internal argument (Jesus functions as the theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state), and the passive construction indicates the sole argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual boundary transition (piercing/penetrating) without a durative process phase; the result state (being impaled) is achieved instantaneously rather than through gradual culmination.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (being pierced/affixed), represents a definable threshold crossing, and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a bounded, telic event with an inherent endpoint (the state of being impaled), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity and lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been impaled' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-impaled to impaled condition), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect and denotes a change-of-state event (the action of being impaled) with a clear boundary and dynamic unfolding, incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' presents Jesus as having actually undergone impalement in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' entails a change of state (Jesus being impaled/crucified) where that resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life history event.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been impaled' is a stative copula describing a resulting condition rather than encoding a transition; it reports Jesus's state without asserting that the resulting state currently holds at the reference time in the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the impalement of Jesus) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; there is no duration adverbial or indication of a non-terminating state extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (impalement as a completed whole) rather than an ongoing process, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Jesus has undergone impalement as a completed fact), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('impaled'), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'Jesus' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by impalement), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: » He is not here , for he has risen just as he promised . Come ; see the place where the Lord lay .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb describing a state change without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/directed motion event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument (agent/initiator).
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (from a state of being dead/lying to a state of being risen) without a durative process phase; it is an achievement marking an instantaneous change-of-state rather than an accomplishment with a measurable duration directed toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, lacking measurable internal duration, and construed as occurring at a definable temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a completed change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from lying to risen position), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from being dead/laid to being risen/alive), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive) involving a dynamic transition with an internal boundary, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has risen' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the actual world (the resurrection is asserted as fact), not as contrary to fact, and contains no counterfactual marking.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a change of location/state (from dead to risen) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life history event.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen' describes a change of location (from lying dead to risen/ascended) where the mover has moved to a destination and the resulting state (being risen/no longer in the tomb) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by 'He is not here' and the invitation to 'see the place where the Lord lay.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the resurrection) that occurred at a specific past point, not a situation holding continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is a present perfect that asserts a realized fact in the actual world (he has completed the action of rising); it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional construction, or hypothetical marking, and thus does not instantiate irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The verb 'has risen' lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle'; 'risen' is the past participle of the intransitive verb 'rise', and the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent/undergoer of the change-of-state, not a passive construction.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb describing a state change or activity without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a directed motion/change-of-location event (rising from the dead) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a transition from dead to alive, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (from dead to alive state) without a durative process phase; it is a near-instantaneous change-of-state that does not involve a measurable duration leading toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has risen at dawn'), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing that either occurs or does not occur.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive state), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and the situation is not homogeneous.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive/resurrected), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive) involving a dynamic transition with an internal boundary, not a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has risen from the dead' as an actual event that has occurred in the world of the narrative, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, and this state is narratively relevant for the subsequent discourse ('he goes before you to Galilee').
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state (being alive/risen) persists at the reference time, and the immediate context ('See, he goes before you') confirms the current effect of this transition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (resurrection from the dead) representing a transition into a new state, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is a present perfect that asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'risen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (the one performing the action of rising); there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle), and the subject is not the semantic patient.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have told' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have told' licenses an internal argument ('you' as the direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'tell' denotes a punctual speech act that achieves its result instantaneously upon utterance, lacking a durative process phase that unfolds toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into awareness (the moment of communication when information enters the recipient's knowledge), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('as I told you at that moment'), and represents a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative act with a specific result state (the disciples having been informed), not a dynamic durative situation; it naturally fits 'in X time' (told in one hour) rather than 'for X time', and represents a bounded, punctual event rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have told' entails a resulting state (the disciples now possess the information that was communicated) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative event with a definite endpoint (the act of telling), which is a bounded eventive action involving dynamic change, not a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' presents a situation that actually occurred in the past (a prior telling), not a counterfactual condition, and contains no markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have told' refers to a prior communication whose resulting state (the disciples now possessing the information) remains relevant for the current discourse context, functioning as narrative setup rather than experiential life history.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have told' merely reports a past interaction (prior instruction) without entailing that the information is currently binding or in effect; the clause does not indicate a persisting resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' describes a discrete past communicative event (telling) rather than a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have told' presents a completed speech act as a bounded whole, functioning as a discrete prior event in the narrative sequence that establishes what was previously communicated to the addressee.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' asserts a realized past action ('as I have told you') in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have told' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'told' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have told' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one doing the telling) and 'you' as the grammatical object (the semantic patient); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has been given' is morphologically passive and licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'All authority'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been given'), and the lexical verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the suppressed agent who gave the authority), making it unaccusative-incompatible.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been given' is passive voice licensing an internal argument ('All authority' as theme/patient receiving the action), thus failing the requirement of no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has been given' denotes a punctual transfer or boundary transition (the moment authority passes to Jesus) without a durative process phase leading to completion, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the transfer of authority), which is a definable threshold event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a completed transfer of authority with an inherent endpoint and result state, not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activity classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been given' entails a resulting change of state (authority transitioning from not possessed to possessed by Jesus), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been') combined with a change-of-state verb ('given'), denoting a bounded event of transfer/bestowal with an internal endpoint, which entails dynamic unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All authority has been given to me' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (Jesus stating a fact about his authority), not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional, inverted conditional, or unfulfilled wish construction.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been given' entails a change of possession/status (authority transferred to Jesus) that results in a persisting state at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for resulting states that hold at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been given' describes a change of possession/authority (from not having to having), and the clause entails that this resulting state (Jesus possessing all authority) currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transfer of authority (a discrete bounded event with focus on the transition into a new state) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (the giving of authority as a completed whole) with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is a present perfect passive asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (authority has been given to Jesus), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been given' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been given' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'give', with the grammatical subject 'All authority' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity affected by the giving event), satisfying the VcPass feature.

FullText: » Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you . Behold , I am with you always , even to the end of the age . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' introduces an overt grammatical subject (I) and an overt direct object (you), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from agent to patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('all the things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an internal argument ('you' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'command' denotes a punctual speech act that achieves its result instantaneously upon utterance, lacking a durative process phase that unfolds toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a durative speech act of issuing orders over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it does not represent a single boundary moment but an extended communicative process, and it naturally permits the durative reading 'finish commanding.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (the issuance of commands as a bounded, perfective event), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have commanded' entails a resulting state (the commands now exist as binding directives) and does not construe as a single punctual occurrence, failing both criteria for semelfactive classification.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have commanded' denotes a completed action with resultant state, marking a change-of-state event (the issuing of commands) rather than a static condition that holds without dynamic development.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have commanded you' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the speaker has genuinely issued commands), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' entails a change in the addressee's knowledge/obligation state (they now know what has been commanded), and this resulting state of the commands remaining in effect is relevant for the subsequent instruction to 'teach them to observe' these commands.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' describes a change of social/legal status (issuing binding directives) where the commands remain currently in effect as binding instructions that the addressees are to observe.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have commanded you' describes a completed action of issuing commands in the past, not a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time; there is no duration adverbial or indication of continuous habitual activity across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' presents a completed transition event (the act of commanding) as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' asserts a realized action in the actual world (past perfect indicating a completed action), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have commanded' lacks the passive auxiliary 'been' required for VcPass morphology; the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one doing the commanding), and 'you' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient, making this active voice, not passive.

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb with only an overt subject ('The time'), matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'The time' undergoes displacement as an internal argument (theme), and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event where the sole argument 'time' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing the event of arrival, not as an external argument (agent/initiator) performing an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (arrival at a temporal point) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/occurrence of 'the time'), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'The time has come at last'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual, bounded event marking the arrival/onset of a specific moment in time, with an inherent endpoint and result state (the time has arrived), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the time has arrived/is now present), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state event marking the arrival/onset of a temporal boundary ('the time has come'), which involves dynamic change and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The time has come' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the kingdom of God is at hand), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional structures.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of state (the time arriving/being present) where that resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life history event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' describes a change of state (arrival/occurrence of time) where the resulting state—that the time is now present—holds at the reference time, as confirmed by the immediately following clause 'The kingdom of God is at hand.'
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of a time/moment) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence of time) construed as a completed bounded whole, presenting a single occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is a present perfect that asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the time has arrived); it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional construction, or hypothetical marking, and thus does not instantiate irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is an active voice construction where the grammatical subject 'The time' is the semantic agent (the entity that comes/arrives), not the patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » What do you want with us Jesus of Nazareth ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' does not introduce an overt internal argument; the infinitive 'to destroy' is a purposive adjunct, not a direct object of 'come', and 'come' itself licenses no affected patient as a grammatical argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') that performs the action of coming with the purpose to destroy, making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing movement to a location, not an external argument initiating an activity without an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location) without a durative process phase; the infinitive 'to destroy' expresses purpose rather than the event structure itself, and 'come' naturally takes 'for X time' (durative) rather than 'in X time' (bounded completion) modification.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/moment), naturally modified by time-point adverbials, and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The infinitive 'to destroy' expresses a bounded purposive endpoint, and 'have come to destroy' denotes a single completed arrival event with an inherent goal, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The infinitive 'to destroy' entails a resulting change of state (transition from non-destroyed to destroyed), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status regardless of the punctual construal of 'have come'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state and dynamic event involving movement and arrival, which involves internal eventive unfolding and progression rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' is an embedded interrogative introduced by 'have' (auxiliary in a yes/no question), not a counterfactual conditional; it expresses uncertainty about the actual purpose of Jesus's arrival, not a situation contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' presents the event as a transition into a new state (arrival with destructive purpose) where the resulting state of being present with that intent is relevant to the immediate discourse context, not as a life experience entry.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival at a location, but the infinitive 'to destroy' expresses purpose/intention rather than a resulting state that holds at reference time; the clause does not entail that destruction has occurred or that a changed state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (coming to destroy) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have come' is a transition event (arrival/coming) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative 'Have you come to destroy us?' asserts a realized fact about the addressee's actual arrival and purpose, not a projected or hypothetical situation; the question seeks confirmation of an actual state of affairs in the narrative world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have come' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (the one performing the action of coming), not the patient.

FullText: He got up , took the bed and went out before them all . They were all filled with wonder . They gave glory to God and said we have never seen anything like this .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('we') and an overt direct object ('anything'), satisfying the condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'we') and takes a direct object ('anything like this'), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('anything like this'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase; the negation 'never seen' marks the absence of a single instantaneous event of perception rather than an incomplete durative process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (perceiving something for the first time), naturally modified by the time-point adverbial 'never' indicating a boundary condition, and lacks measurable internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something for the first time), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have never seen' entails a resulting state of knowledge/experience and unfolds over an extended duration (a lifetime of not seeing), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (perceiving something for the first time), which encodes eventive change rather than a static stative condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' presents an actual state in the world (the speakers' genuine experience of not having witnessed such an event), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the negative Perfect 'have never seen' with the explicit frequency adverb 'never', which functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, meeting the set_to_1_if criteria.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause reports a past experiential fact without indicating that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' expresses a negative universal state extending from the past to the reference time, indicating a situation (non-occurrence) that has held continuously throughout the entire relevant time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' is negated with 'never', which indicates the transition has not occurred at the reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' asserts a realized state in the actual world (a past experience of the speakers), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; negation alone does not trigger irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have never seen' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'we' as the grammatical subject and semantic agent.

FullText: Jesus heard this . He said to them : » Those who are well have no need of a medical man but those who are ill do . I have come to care for sinners not the upright . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' introduces an overt internal argument ('to care for sinners'), realized as an infinitival complement that encodes the affected/directed purpose of the coming event, satisfying the transitive criterion of an external argument (I) directing an event toward an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a purposive infinitival complement 'to care for sinners', indicating the verb encodes a volitional action with an external argument rather than a one-participant unaccusative event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing movement to a location, not an external argument initiating an activity without an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase; it marks the completion of motion as a single transition event rather than an unfolding process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or assumption of a role), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'I have come at this moment'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a completed arrival with an inherent endpoint and purposive result state (to care for sinners), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being present to being present for a purpose) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence but rather as a completed action with consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state and bounded event marking the onset of arrival, involving dynamic progression from one location/state to another, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have come to care for sinners' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (Jesus stating his actual purpose), with no indication of counterfactual condition, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact marking.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have come' entails a change of location (Jesus arriving/coming), and the resulting state of being present is relevant to the narrative context where Jesus explains his purpose, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history claim.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' indicates that Jesus has moved to a destination (among sinners) and remains there at the reference time, with the resulting state of his presence persisting to accomplish his stated purpose of caring for sinners.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have come to care for sinners' describes a discrete bounded event (arrival/transition into a purpose) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have come' is a transition event (arrival/movement to a location) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with an asserted endpoint in the narrative.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is asserted as a realized fact within Jesus's direct speech declaring his actual purpose and mission, not as projected, hypothetical, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle used in active perfect aspect with 'I' as the semantic agent (the one who came), not the semantic patient.

FullText: When his own people heard of this they went to apprehend him . They said : » He has lost his senses . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has lost' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('He') and an overt direct object ('his senses'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'lost' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') and introduces a direct object ('his senses'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has lost' licenses an internal argument ('his senses' as direct object), therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'lose' in 'has lost his senses' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition into a state of mental incapacity) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for achievement verbs.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (loss of senses/sanity), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has lost at that moment'), does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from having senses to lacking them), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has lost his senses' entails a resulting change of state (transition from having senses to having lost them), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it is a perfective event with a consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has lost' denotes a change-of-state event (loss of senses) involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which violates the stative requirement of lacking internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has lost his senses' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the narrative world (the people's assessment of what is true about him), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has lost' entails a change of state (loss of sanity/senses) where that resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life history event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has lost' describes a change of state (loss of mental faculties) where the resulting state (being senseless/irrational) is currently held at the reference time, as evidenced by the speakers' present assessment of his condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'He has lost his senses' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a new state of mental condition) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'lose' is a transition event (change of state from having senses to lacking them), and 'has lost' presents this transition as a completed whole bounded at a prior point, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has lost' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (he has lost his senses), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has lost' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'lost', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has lost' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'lose'), with the grammatical subject 'He' functioning as the semantic agent (the one who lost his senses), not the patient; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » If Satan has risen up against himself , he is divided , cannot stand and will come to an end .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'against himself' is a prepositional phrase indicating direction/opposition, not a grammatical object, so the transitive feature is not instantiated.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'rise up' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Satan) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen up' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (Satan) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state/position rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a punctual boundary transition (movement from a state of not-risen to risen) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement profile rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from not-risen to risen), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has risen up at dawn'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has risen up' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (the completion of rising), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and the situation is not homogeneous across subintervals.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen up' entails a transition into a new resulting state (Satan moving from a non-risen to a risen position), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a change-of-state event involving dynamic progression from a lower to higher position, which constitutes eventive unfolding incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents an open conditional where the truth of the condition remains possible (Satan may or may not have risen up), not a counterfactual situation understood not to have occurred.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' entails a change of state (Satan moving from not-risen to risen), and the resulting state of being risen/in opposition holds at the reference time, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history experience.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen up' describes a change of state/position (Satan moving from a lower to higher state of opposition), and the conditional structure indicates this resulting state of being 'risen up against himself' is currently in effect at the reference time, satisfying the resultative feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' denotes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a state of rebellion) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen up' is a transition event (rise up = change of state/position) construed as a completed whole in a conditional protasis, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than ongoing development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen up' appears in a conditional construction ('If Satan has risen up...') that projects a hypothetical scenario rather than asserting a realized fact, triggering irrealis marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen up' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'risen up', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen up' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); 'risen' is the past participle of an intransitive verb, and the subject 'Satan' is the semantic agent/doer, not the patient.

FullText: » These are they by the wayside where the word is sown . When they have heard ; Satan comes and takes away the word that has been sown in them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') and an overt direct object ('the word'), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'hear' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they' as agent/experiencer) and takes an internal argument (the word as the object of perception), thus it is not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (experiencer/agent of the hearing event) with no internal argument (direct object) realized in the clause.
