AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' denotes a completed, punctual communicative event with a defined endpoint (the act of speaking finished), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had spoken' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of speech with consequent understanding/communication achieved) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' denotes a completed dynamic action involving change-of-state and eventive unfolding (the act of speaking), which lacks the static, unchanging quality required for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had spoken' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (he spoke, then was received into heaven), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'had spoken to them' sets up a narrative sequence where the resulting state (having communicated) is relevant for the subsequent plot point ('the Lord Jesus was received up into heaven'), making this narrative progression non-experiential rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had spoken' describes a communication event that occurred prior to the main events (Jesus being received into heaven); it does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time, as speaking is a completed action without an ongoing consequential state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (speaking to them) that serves as a temporal boundary for subsequent events (being received up into heaven), not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had spoken' presents the speech act as a single bounded, completed event that serves as a discrete temporal landmark ('After he had spoken') in the narrative sequence, construing the transition into the state of having spoken as a whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had spoken' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (the Lord Jesus spoke to them before being received into heaven); it is not marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical, and the narrative context presents it as a completed fact rather than a projected or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'had spoken' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle of intransitive 'speak'), not the passive construction 'had been spoken'; the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one doing the speaking), not the patient.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'attempted' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Many') and an overt internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to write an orderly narrative'), satisfying the transitive pattern where the event is directed from the external argument toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'many' who perform the action of attempting), and the clause contains an overt infinitival object ('to write'), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'attempted' takes a subject ('Many') functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in a volitional activity with no internal argument licensed—the infinitival complement 'to write' is not an object argument but rather a purposive adjunct.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'attempt' denotes a punctual transition into the state of trying/endeavoring without an inherent endpoint or result state; it lacks the durative process phase culminating in a defined terminal point required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'attempt' denotes an ongoing effort or process that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish attempting'), indicating a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'attempted' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the attempt itself is completed), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot naturally take 'for X time' without implying iterative attempts, and the situation is not homogeneous.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'attempted' entails a transition into a resulting state (having made an attempt) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'attempted' denotes a dynamic, bounded event involving volitional effort and internal progression toward a goal, which entails eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not happen.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' denotes a bounded activity (attempting to write) where the focus is on whether the experience has occurred in the subject's life history rather than on a resulting state, fitting the experiential perfect interpretation.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'attempt' describes an activity or effort without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause reports a past action without indicating that a changed state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (multiple separate attempts to write narratives) rather than a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' presents the attempt as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, construing the transition into the state of having made an attempt as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state in the actual world (people have actually attempted this), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'attempted', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have attempted' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'attempted' as the main verb and the grammatical subject 'Many' as the semantic agent performing the action.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'take place' does not introduce an overt internal argument; it is an intransitive verb expressing an event without a direct or indirect object, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an occurrence/happening event where the sole argument ('things') functions as an internal argument and no external argument is licensed, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence, appearance, or occurrence verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a change-of-state or occurrence event where the sole argument 'things' functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, and the verb does not license a volitional agent as external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous boundary transition (the occurrence of an event) without a durative process phase, and does not naturally accept 'in X time' modification to measure completion of a bounded process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'have taken place' denotes a punctual transition into a state of occurrence; events either take place or do not, and the phrase naturally accepts specific time-point modification (e.g., 'took place at noon'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have taken place' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the occurrence of events), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have taken place' entails a transition into a resulting state (things now exist in a completed state) and is construed as a durative situation encompassing multiple events ('things'), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a dynamic event with internal eventive unfolding and change, marking the occurrence of situations rather than a static state that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that have taken place' presents events as having actually occurred in the actual world within a narrative context, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have taken place' denotes bounded events (things occurring) where the focus is on whether these events have occurred in the subject's history rather than on a resulting state, fitting the experiential perfect category.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' (occur/happen) is an activity verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports that events occurred in the past without indicating a current state of affairs.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'things that have taken place' describes discrete bounded events rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating a state extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have taken place' is a transition event (things coming into occurrence) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situations as bounded occurrences rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have taken place' is in the present perfect tense asserting realized events in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have taken place' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'taken' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have taken place' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'taken place' is a phrasal verb functioning as a single lexical unit, not a passive construction with 'been' as the passive auxiliary.

FullText: Most excellent Theophilus Greek : Theo philos : friend of God : I have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning . Now it seems good to me to write this report to you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'traced' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('the course of all things'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'traced' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent) and introduces a direct object ('the course of all things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for transitive verbs with overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'traced' licenses an internal argument ('the course of all things'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'traced' denotes a durative investigative process directed toward a defined goal (comprehensive understanding of 'the course of all things from the beginning'), which naturally admits 'in X time' modification and involves gradual accumulation of knowledge culminating in a complete result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'traced' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition of having investigated/understood 'the course of all things,' construed as a completed boundary event rather than an unfolding process, satisfying the achievement criterion for perceptual/cognitive transitions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'traced' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completing the tracing of 'the course of all things from the beginning'), making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have traced' entails a resulting state of completion (having thoroughly investigated and understood the course of all things), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have traced' denotes a completed dynamic action involving internal eventive unfolding (the process of tracing/investigating), which is a change-of-state event with a clear endpoint, not a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality; it is a statement of fact about what the speaker has done.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have traced' entails a change of epistemic state (the speaker has now acquired knowledge of the course of all things), and this resulting state of having completed the investigation holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a claim about life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'traced' describes an investigative activity (following a course of events) rather than a change of state; the resulting state of having traced does not persist as a meaningful condition at the reference time, and the clause reports a completed past action without entailing a persisting resultant state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed discrete action of tracing (a bounded accomplishment) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning' presents a completed investigative action as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, matching the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have accurately traced' is in the present perfect tense asserting a completed action as a realized fact in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have traced' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action, and 'the course of all things' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' between 'have' and the past participle 'traced'.

FullText: » Have no fear Zechariah , « said the angel , » for God has heard your prayer . Your wife Elisabeth will have a son and his name will be John .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('God') and an overt direct object ('your prayer'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has heard' licenses an external argument (God as agent) and introduces an internal argument (your prayer as the direct object), making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has heard' licenses an internal argument ('your prayer' as the direct object), thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has heard' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase; it marks the boundary moment of perceiving/becoming aware of the prayer, not a gradual change-of-state or directed process culminating in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (God becoming aware of the prayer at a definable moment), which is a classic achievement verb that can be modified by time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the prayer), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard the prayer), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of the cognitive act of hearing/perceiving the prayer, which is an eventive change rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'God has heard your prayer' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (God's hearing of the prayer is a real event), with no indication that it is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has heard' entails a change of state (God's awareness/attention to the prayer) that holds at the reference time and is relevant for the subsequent discourse (the promise of a son), making it non-experiential rather than a life history claim.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has heard' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past perceptual event without indicating that a changed state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'God has heard your prayer' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of hearing a specific prayer) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'God has heard your prayer' presents the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint (the completion of the hearing event), construing the transition into the state of having-heard as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has heard' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (God's action of hearing the prayer), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has heard' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'heard', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'God' as the semantic agent (the one who hears) and the object 'your prayer' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » He will go before God in the spirit and power of Elijah . He will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and wrongdoers to the way of righteousness . This will make ready a people whose hearts have been directed to Jehovah . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have been directed' is in passive voice and licenses both an external argument (implied agent causing the direction) and an internal argument (the overt direct object 'hearts'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target verb 'have been directed' is in passive voice and licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the directing action), thus failing the unaccusative criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'directed' licenses an internal argument ('hearts') functioning as the theme/patient of the passive construction, and the sole overt argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'directed' denotes a punctual change-of-state (hearts transitioning into a directed/oriented condition toward Jehovah) without an inherent durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have been directed to Jehovah' denotes a state resulting from a durative process of spiritual orientation or guidance rather than a punctual transition, and it naturally permits the complement 'finish' (one can 'finish directing hearts'), indicating a durative process rather than an instantaneous boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have been directed to Jehovah' denotes a bounded result state (hearts oriented toward Jehovah) rather than a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific endpoint and lacks the homogeneous, ongoing character required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been directed to Jehovah' entails a resulting change of state (hearts transitioning from undirected to directed toward Jehovah), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been directed' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('have been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and change-of-state (hearts being directed/oriented toward Jehovah), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'whose hearts have been directed to Jehovah' presents a situation as actually occurring (a future state that will be realized), not as contrary to fact; it is part of a predictive statement about what 'will make ready a people,' with no counterfactual marking or indication that the event did not/will not occur.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been directed' entails a change of state (hearts being oriented toward Jehovah) that persists at the reference time as a defining characteristic of the prepared people, rather than marking a mere occurrence in life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been directed' describes a change of state (hearts being oriented/aimed toward Jehovah) where the resulting state of being directed to Jehovah currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the resultative feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a resultant state achieved through preparation ('This will make ready a people whose hearts have been directed'), focusing on the transition into that state rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'whose hearts have been directed to Jehovah' presents a passive bounded transition where the hearts are construed as having undergone a completed directional change, fitting the perfective aspect profile of a transition event asserted as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been directed' asserts a realized state of affairs (hearts that are directed to Jehovah) within a relative clause describing an actual people, without modal, conditional, future, or wish marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been directed' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been directed' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'directed' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'whose hearts' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by the directing), and the agent is expressed in a to-phrase ('to Jehovah').

FullText: The angel answered : » I am Gabriel , whose place is before God . I have been sent to give you this good news .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have been sent' is morphologically passive and licenses both an external argument (agent, implied) and an internal argument (patient: 'you'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been sent'), and the lexical verb 'send' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent 'by God' or similar), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with licensed external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been sent' is passive voice where the subject 'I' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) receiving the action rather than as an external argument (agent), and the verb licenses an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sent' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of dispatch) without a durative process phase; the perfect aspect marks completion of this instantaneous change-of-state rather than a gradual culminating process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have been sent' denotes a punctual transition into the state of being sent (a change from not-sent to sent), which is a definable threshold transition that can occur at a specific temporal boundary and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been sent' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the act of being sent to deliver news), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been sent' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-sent to sent condition) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been sent' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect, which entails dynamic eventive unfolding (the action of sending), and the verb 'send' denotes a change-of-state event with an internal boundary, both incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have been sent to give you this good news' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the narrative world (the angel states a fact about his current mission), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been sent' entails a change of state/location (the angel was sent to a location/purpose) with a resulting state that holds at the reference time (the angel is present to deliver the message), making this non-experiential rather than a life history event.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been sent' describes a change of state (from not-sent to sent) with a resulting state that persists at the reference time, as indicated by the purpose clause 'to give you this good news' which shows the angel is currently in the state of having been sent with an active mission.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (being sent) with focus on the transition into that state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been sent' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (the act of being sent) as a completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been sent' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the angel has completed the action of being sent), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been sent' includes 'been' followed by a past participle (progressive perfect form), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive marking in perfect constructions.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been sent' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'sent' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'I' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity receiving the sending), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » You will be without voice or language till the day when these things happen . Your lack of faith in what I say has caused this . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has caused' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Your lack of faith in what I say') and an overt direct object ('this'), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'caused' licenses an external argument (agent: 'Your lack of faith') and introduces an internal argument (theme: 'this'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'caused' licenses an internal argument ('this'), which functions as the theme/patient of the causation event, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'cause' denotes a punctual transition into a result state (the causing of the condition) without a durative process phase; it marks a boundary event rather than an accomplishment with an inherent temporal unfolding toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'caused' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the condition of being without voice/language), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('has caused this at that moment'), and represents a definable threshold event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'caused' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint and result state (the causing of the loss of voice/language), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has caused' entails a resulting change of state (loss of voice/language), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status per the set_to_0_if rule.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'caused' denotes a change-of-state event involving dynamic progression from a prior state (having faith) to a resulting state (loss of voice), which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Your lack of faith in what I say has caused this' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the lack of faith did cause the loss of voice/language), with no indication that it is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'caused' entails a change of state (the subject's loss of voice/language), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, making this a resultative perfect rather than an experiential one.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'caused' describes a change of state (loss of voice/language) with the resulting state currently in effect at the reference time, as indicated by the present tense context 'Your lack of faith...has caused this' where the consequence persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Your lack of faith in what I say has caused this' describes a discrete bounded event (the causation of a specific condition) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Your lack of faith in what I say has caused this' presents 'cause' as a bounded transition event with a construed endpoint (the current state of being without voice/language), asserting a completed whole rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has caused this' uses present perfect to assert a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a completed action with present relevance), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has caused' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'caused' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Your lack of faith in what I say' corresponds to the semantic agent (causer), and 'this' is the semantic patient (object affected); the construction lacks passive morphology (no 'have been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: She said : » This is the way God has dealt with me by taking away my reproach among men . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('God') and an overt internal argument ('me'), satisfying the criterion for a two-participant transitive construction with an external and internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' licenses an external argument (God as agent) and introduces an internal argument (me as the object of the preposition 'with'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' licenses an internal argument ('me'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'dealt with' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous action of treatment/handling without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' to indicate bounded completion, and the event is construed as a single completed action rather than a gradual culmination.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' denotes an extended process of handling or managing a situation rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it unfolds over measurable duration and cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial like 'at noon'.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has dealt with me by taking away my reproach' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (removal of reproach), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has dealt with me' entails a resulting change of state (taking away reproach), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; it describes a transition into a new state rather than a punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'dealt with' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event (God's action of removing reproach), involving internal eventive unfolding and progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has dealt with me' as an actual occurrence in the real world (God's action of removing reproach), not as a counterfactual or contrary-to-fact situation.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' entails a change of state (removal of reproach) that persists at the reference time, making this a resultative perfect rather than an experiential one.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' describes a change of state (removal of reproach) that results in a current condition (reproach is now gone), and the clause indicates this resulting state is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (God's action of taking away reproach) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual series present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has dealt with' is construed as a completed transition event (a bounded action with a construed endpoint), presenting God's action as a single accomplished whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has dealt with' is a present perfect that asserts a realized action in the actual world within the speaker's reported statement; it is not marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has dealt with' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'dealt', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'God' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action, and 'me' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle structure), so this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: The angel said to Mary : » Do not fear for you have found favor with God .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'found' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('favor'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'found' licenses an external argument (you/Mary as agent) and introduces a direct object (favor), violating the unaccusative criteria which require no external argument and no object.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'found' licenses an internal argument ('favor'), which is the direct object receiving the action, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.

FullText: » Your relative Elisabeth has also conceived a son even in her old age . This so-called barren woman is in her sixth month .

FullText: » He has looked on the low position of his servant . All generations from now on will call me blessed .

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'looked on' denotes perception/observation where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (God's act of regarding/noticing the servant's low position) as part of the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state that persists at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'looked on' is a perception/observation verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past act of regarding or noticing without a change of state that currently holds.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has looked on the low position of his servant' presents a completed transition event (the act of looking/regarding) as a bounded whole, construed as a single occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has done great things' is a Perfect form denoting a bounded activity (doing great things) that focuses on the occurrence of the experience in the subject's life history rather than on a persisting resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'has done great things' describes a completed action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it reports a past accomplishment rather than a transition to a state currently in effect.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has done great things' uses present perfect to assert a realized action in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'help' does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it describes an action or activity without encoding a transition to a new state that currently holds.

FullText: » Blessed be Jehovah , the God of Israel . He has visited his people and brought them redemption .

DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'brought redemption' denotes a discrete bounded event (a completed action of delivering redemption) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has brought' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (redemption has been accomplished), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: The angels went away from them into heaven . Then the shepherds said to one another , » Let us go to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened , which God told us about . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'happened' denotes an occurrence/event without licensing an external argument; the sole argument 'thing' functions as an internal argument (theme), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence, appearance, or occurrence verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'happened' denotes a change-of-state or coming-into-being event where the sole argument ('thing') functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'happened' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous transition into a state of occurrence without a durative process phase, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'happened' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (an event occurring at a definable temporal boundary), lacks measurable internal duration, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.

AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has happened' denotes a change-of-state event with a clear boundary marking the onset of a situation, which is incompatible with AktState; the perfect aspect here emphasizes the completion of a dynamic event rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that has happened' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the narrative world (the shepherds witnessed the angels and now refer to the actual event that took place), with no indication of counterfactuality.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'happen' does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it describes a completed event without encoding a transition to a new state that currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'that has happened' describes a discrete bounded event (the birth/event at Bethlehem) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has happened' presents a transition event (the occurrence of 'this thing') as a single bounded, completed whole, fitting the perfective construal of a discrete event in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'that has happened' asserts a realized event in the actual world (the shepherds refer to an event that God told them about), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has happened' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'happened' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had heard' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (what they had previously heard about the child); it occurs in a reported speech context without modal, conditional, or hypothetical marking, thus instantiating realis rather than irrealis.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had heard' is a perception verb in a negative Perfect-like construction ('all the things that they had heard and seen') that focuses on the occurrence of experiences in the shepherds' life history rather than on a resulting state.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (perceiving something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had seen' is a perception verb in a Perfect construction focusing on the occurrence of the experience (what the shepherds witnessed) rather than a resulting state, fitting the experiential interpretation of DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past experience without a change of state or condition currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things heard and seen) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (the shepherds' prior experience of seeing), embedded in a relative clause describing factual events, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

FullText: » My eyes have seen your salvation .

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (perceiving/witnessing salvation), which is a momentary crossing of a threshold from not-seeing to seeing, meeting the criterion for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing your salvation), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have seen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having seen to having seen salvation) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of visual awareness (onset of seeing), which encodes eventive change rather than a static stative condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'My eyes have seen your salvation' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (witnessing salvation) at least once in the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause reports a past perceptual experience without indicating that a change of state currently holds.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have seen' presents a bounded completion of the perceptual transition event (moving from not-seeing to seeing), construed as a single accomplished whole rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is a present perfect that asserts a realized state in the actual world (a completed action with present relevance), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, future, or wish context present.

FullText: She had been a widow eighty-four years . She never left the temple . She worshipped and fasted with supplications day and night .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been' functions as a copula linking the subject 'She' to the predicative complement 'a widow', introducing no internal argument and thus failing to encode a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole overt argument 'She' functions as the syntactic subject linked to the predicative complement 'a widow'; the verb does not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula) with predicate 'a widow' denotes a state of existence/appearance rather than a one-participant event with an external argument; the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) in a change-of-state construction, not as an agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The state 'had been a widow' denotes a stative condition without an inherent durative process directed toward a defined endpoint; it describes a prolonged state of being rather than an event that unfolds toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been a widow' denotes a durative state maintained over eighty-four years rather than a punctual transition, and the past perfect progressive construction emphasizes the extended duration of the state rather than a boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been a widow' denotes a stative condition with an inherent endpoint (widowhood began at a specific point), lacks dynamic unfolding, and does not require sustained dynamic engagement to continue, thus failing all criteria for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been a widow' entails a resulting state (widowhood) that persists over eighty-four years, making it a durative state rather than a punctual semelfactive event.
AktState_explanation: The gram is morphologically marked as progressive perfect ('had been'), which entails dynamic unfolding over time and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been a widow eighty-four years' presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (she was a widow for 84 years), using past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative without any counterfactual marking, contrastive discourse, or conditional structure.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been a widow' entails a change of social/legal status (transition to widowhood) that persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the duration adverbial 'eighty-four years' indicating the continuing state.
DtRes_explanation: The copular construction 'had been a widow' describes a stative condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a persisting state over a duration ('eighty-four years'), not a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had been a widow eighty-four years' expresses a state (widowhood) that held continuously from a past starting point through a specified duration (eighty-four years) to the reference time, matching the intuition of a situation extending continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been a widow eighty-four years' denotes a stative situation (widow status) with duration adverbial 'eighty-four years' that makes internal temporal span accessible rather than presenting the situation as a bounded whole, and lacks a construed transition or endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been a widow' uses past perfect to assert a realized state of affairs in the actual world (she was a widow for eighty-four years), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.

VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun phrase 'a widow' (non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: When they had accomplished all things that were according to the law of God they returned to Galilee , to their own city Nazareth .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had accomplished' introduces an overt grammatical subject (they) and an overt direct object (all things), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they') and takes a direct object ('all things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' licenses an internal argument ('all things'), which functions as a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' denotes a durative process directed toward a defined goal (completing all required things according to the law), naturally modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'had accomplished all things in three weeks'), and culminates in a result state where the tasks are complete.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' denotes a durative process of completing multiple actions ('all things'), not a punctual transition into a new state; it naturally permits the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish accomplishing') and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring at a temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had accomplished' denotes a bounded, telic situation with an inherent endpoint (completion of 'all things according to the law of God'), which entails a specific result state and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying goal completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had accomplished all things' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of tasks according to the law) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' denotes a change-of-state event with a clear endpoint and internal eventive unfolding (moving from not-accomplished to accomplished), which is incompatible with AktState.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had accomplished' entails completion of a bounded task, and the resulting state (all things accomplished) is relevant to the subsequent action ('they returned'), making this narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'accomplish' describes a completed activity/task, not a change of state that persists at reference time; the clause uses 'when' to mark a temporal boundary for a subsequent action (returning), indicating the accomplishment is a prerequisite event rather than a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (accomplishing all things according to the law) that serves as a temporal boundary for the subsequent action (returning to Galilee), rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause presents 'had accomplished all things' as a single bounded completion with a construed endpoint, functioning as a discrete narrative step that precedes the main action ('they returned'), and the predicate 'accomplish' is a transition event construed as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had accomplished' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world within a temporal clause ('When they had accomplished...'), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future construction.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'had accomplished' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent (doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required auxiliary sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have searched for you' contains the duration adverbial 'for you' (implying a span of time) and is coordinated with 'have been worried', which profiles an ongoing state rather than a bounded transition, making the internal temporal development accessible rather than presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have searched' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world (the mother and father did search for the son), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have been worried' denotes a stative condition (emotional state) that lacks an inherent endpoint or result state; it does not involve a durative process directed toward completion, and naturally takes 'for X time' (worried for days) rather than 'in X time' adverbials.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been worried' entails a resulting state of worry that persists over time and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been worried' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (present perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings according to the set_to_0_if criteria.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been worried' entails a persisting emotional state that holds at the reference time (the mother is still worried as she speaks), not merely the occurrence of worry as a life experience.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'have been worried' is a stative copula (present perfect of 'be' + adjective) that describes an ongoing emotional state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; per edge case guidance, copular constructions do not encode the change itself but only the state.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been worried' denotes a continuous state of worry extending from a past starting point (when the son went missing) to the reference time (the present moment of speaking), without explicit temporal bounding.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been worried' profiles an ongoing emotional state without a construed endpoint or transition, making it stative rather than presenting a bounded whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been worried' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the mother and father have actually experienced worry during the search), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

FullText: John reproved Herod the tetrarch for all the evil things he had done for he took his brother Herodias’ wife .

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a durative process of committing evil acts over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it naturally permits completion as a process phase (one can 'finish doing evil things'), failing multiple set_to_1 criteria.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he had done' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (Herod's evil actions are real events that happened), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'he had done evil things' presents a resulting state (the evil actions and their consequences) that is relevant to the narrative context of John's reproof, functioning as the basis for the reproof rather than as a life experience entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had done' describes past actions (evil things) without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it reports completed events in the past rather than a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he had done' describes discrete bounded events (evil things) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had done' presents the evil things as a bounded, completed whole event in the past, construed as a single occurrence rather than as ongoing or habitual, fitting the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' asserts a realized past action in the actual world (Herod's evil deeds that actually occurred), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: Then the devil said : » I will give all this authority and glory to you , for it has been turned over to me . I may give it to whomever I will .
