ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you'), making it unergative rather than unaccusative; the sole overt argument functions as the external argument (agent), not an internal argument (theme/patient).
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in the activity of speaking, with no internal argument (direct or indirect object) licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous act of utterance that reaches completion at a single boundary moment, lacking an inherent durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' denotes a durative process of producing utterances over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish speaking'), indicating completion of a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have spoken' denotes a completed speech act with an inherent endpoint (the utterance is finished), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot naturally take 'for X time' without implying an ongoing activity rather than the perfective sense present in the text.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have spoken' construes a completed action with a resulting state (words that were spoken remain spoken and will have consequences), not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have spoken' denotes a completed speech act with a defined endpoint, marking a bounded eventive change rather than a static condition that holds over time without dynamic progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'That which you have spoken in the inner chambers' presents a situation as having actually occurred (past perfect expressing temporal anteriority in narrative), without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' is a perception/communication verb focusing on the occurrence of the experience (speaking in inner chambers) as part of the subject's life history, without asserting a resulting state, matching the experiential perfect feature.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have spoken' describes a communication event (past utterance in inner chambers) without entailing that the spoken words themselves persist in effect at the reference time; the clause reports a past interaction, not a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things spoken in the past) rather than a situation holding continuously to the reference time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating continuous extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' presents the act of speaking as a completed, bounded transition event with a construed endpoint, functioning as a discrete step in the narrative sequence that parallels the preceding clause.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' asserts a realized action in the actual world (what you have already spoken), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have spoken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'spoken' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is active voice (subject 'you' is the semantic agent/speaker), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been + past participle' with the subject as semantic patient.

FullText: » I will show you the one to fear . Fear the one who , after he has killed , has power to destroy you throw into the ever-burning trash fires of the valley of Hinnom Greek : Gehenna . This is one you should fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has killed' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (implied by the relative clause structure where 'the one' is the antecedent being acted upon), satisfying the transitive pattern with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'killed' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he' who performs the killing action), and the clause contains an implicit direct object (the patient/theme being killed), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has killed' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'you' or a general patient), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria of having no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a punctual, near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition from alive to dead) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (death of the victim) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, satisfying the core criterion for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the death of the victim) and result state, not a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has killed' entails a resulting change of state (the victim transitions from alive to dead), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a change-of-state event with a clear boundary (the transition from alive to dead), involving dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after he has killed' presents a situation as having actually occurred (temporal anteriority in a narrative sequence), not as contrary to fact; it describes a real event that precedes the ability to destroy, with no counterfactual marking.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has killed' denotes a change of state (death) with a resulting state that is relevant to the subsequent clause 'has power to destroy you,' making this a narrative progression establishing why the subject should be feared rather than an experiential claim about life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has killed' describes a change of state (transition from alive to dead) where the undergoer remains in that resulting state at the reference time, and the clause structure ('after he has killed, has power to destroy') indicates the killing is a completed transition with a persisting result.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'after he has killed' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of killing) that serves as a temporal boundary for the subsequent state, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has killed' is a transition event (kill = change of state from alive to dead) construed as a completed whole, functioning as a discrete bounded occurrence in the narrative sequence introduced by 'after'.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has killed' asserts a realized past action within a conditional relative clause that describes an actual characteristic of the person being referenced; it is not itself marked as modal, future, hypothetical, or counterfactual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has killed' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'killed' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has killed' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the killing action, not the patient.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had known' introduces an overt internal argument ('in what hour the thief was coming'), satisfying the transitive pattern of an external argument (master) directing an action toward an internal argument (the content clause functioning as the patient/theme).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/experiencer) and takes an internal argument (the complement clause 'in what hour the thief was coming'), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'known' licenses an internal argument (the clause 'in what hour the thief was coming'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'know' in 'had known' denotes a punctual transition into a state of awareness or understanding without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement (set_to_0) profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (crossing the threshold from ignorance to knowledge), which is a characteristic achievement event that can be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('in what hour') and lacks measurable internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had known' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness (knowing in what hour the thief was coming), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had known' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it is a stative verb expressing a durative condition rather than a punctual dynamic occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had known' in a counterfactual conditional context denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the onset of awareness (knowing at a specific moment when the thief was coming), which is eventive rather than a static stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if the master of the house had known'), where the condition is understood not to have been satisfied, paired with the apodosis 'he would have watched' containing a modal perfect, marking the situation as contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had known' functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's (master's) history, fitting the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual (introduced by 'if') and negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (the master knowing the hour of the thief's arrival) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had known' is in a counterfactual conditional protasis ('if...had known') that presents an unrealized, open hypothetical situation rather than a bounded completed event; the clause does not assert a transition as a discrete narrative step but instead construes a stative epistemic condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched'), which presents a hypothetical, non-realized situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is active voice (agent subject 'master' + transitive verb + object 'in what hour'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle morphology of a passive construction.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'would have watched' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt direct object ('his house'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/initiator) and denotes a volitional action that requires an agent, not a change-of-state or motion event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'watched' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in a volitional activity without licensing an internal argument (direct or indirect object).
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' denotes a durative process of vigilant monitoring directed toward the goal of preventing theft, naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'would have watched in that hour'), and involves sustained action culminating in a defined protective result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'watched' denotes a durative process of maintaining vigilance over an extended period rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in this context.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of sustained vigilance that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint; it can be naturally modified by 'for X time' (e.g., 'would have watched for hours') and is homogeneous in nature.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have watched' unfolds over a measurable duration (vigilant monitoring) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, and does not fit the semelfactive profile of instantaneous, non-resultative events like 'knock' or 'sneeze'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'watch' denotes a dynamic activity involving active engagement and internal progression (monitoring/surveillance), which entails eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if the master of the house had known', expressing what would have happened under a contrary-to-fact condition.
DtExp_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' entails a change of state (from not watching to watching/being vigilant) with a resulting state of vigilance that would hold at the reference time, and the clause structure ('if...he would have watched') presents this as a counterfactual consequence rather than an experiential entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical action that did not occur; it does not describe an actual change of state that holds at the reference time, and 'watch' (an activity verb) does not entail a resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete, bounded conditional action (watching to prevent a break-in) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' presents a bounded, completed hypothetical event (a transition into the state of watching/vigilance) as a single whole, construed as a discrete occurrence that would have occurred at a specific time if the condition had been met.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' appears in a conditional construction ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched'), presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical situation rather than a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' includes a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is active voice with 'he' as the semantic agent (the doer of watching), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and does not express the subject as a semantic patient.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'allowed' introduces an overt external argument (implied subject 'he') and an overt internal argument ('his house to be broken into'), satisfying the transitive pattern requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/initiator) and introduces a direct object ('his house to be broken into'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'his house to be broken into'), which functions as the theme/patient of the permission relation, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'allow' denotes a punctual decision or permission event without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary transition into a state of permission rather than an accomplishment with measurable duration toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'allow' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (permitting vs. not permitting), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('would not have allowed at that moment'), and represents a boundary decision point rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a single, bounded decision or permission event with an inherent endpoint (preventing the breaking in), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning and lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'allowed his house to be broken into' entails a resulting change of state (the house being broken into), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'allowed' denotes a dynamic volitional action involving active engagement and control (preventing/permitting an event), which constitutes eventive change rather than a static state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if the master of the house had known', expressing an unfulfilled past situation contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'would not have allowed' expresses a counterfactual conditional about preventing a resulting state (house remaining unbroken), not about the occurrence of an experience in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: This is a counterfactual conditional (would not have allowed) describing a hypothetical past scenario that did not occur; it does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time, as the condition was never met.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete hypothetical event (preventing a house break-in) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating continuous extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a bounded transition event (allowing/not allowing) construed as a completed whole in the hypothetical scenario, matching the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' is a modal auxiliary construction (would + have + allowed) in a counterfactual conditional context ('if the master...had known'), presenting a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than an asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would not have allowed' includes a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' is an active voice construction where the grammatical subject 'He' is the semantic agent (the one doing the allowing/preventing), and 'his house to be broken into' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have + been + past participle'.

FullText: » Do you think I have come to give peace in the earth ? I tell you , No , instead division .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'to give peace' is a purpose clause, not a direct object of 'come'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a purposive infinitival complement ('to give peace'), indicating an agentive event rather than an unaccusative change-of-state or motion event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (I) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement to a location, not as an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location or state) without a durative process phase; it marks the instantaneous crossing of a threshold rather than a gradual culmination, fitting the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or in a situation) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have come at that moment') and represents crossing a definable threshold without internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have come' denotes a completed action with a specific endpoint (arrival at a location/state) and entails a result state, making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being present to being present) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state event (arrival/movement to a location) involving dynamic progression and an inherent endpoint, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have come' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (the speaker is asserting their actual purpose), not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a transition into a new state/purpose (arrival with a specific mission), and the resulting state of having arrived for that purpose is relevant to the discourse that follows ('I tell you, No, instead division'), making this narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes arrival at a destination, but the context indicates the speaker is explaining their purpose/mission rather than asserting that they currently remain at a location, and the clause does not entail a resulting state of being present that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (coming to give peace) with focus on the transition/purpose rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event, and 'have come' presents this transition as a completed bounded whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' appears in a rhetorical question asserting a realized fact about the speaker's purpose, not presenting the situation as hypothetical, projected, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is a past participle but 'been' is absent, and the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one who came), not the semantic patient.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'coming' introduces an overt internal argument 'here' (locative complement functioning as an affected location toward which the action is directed), satisfying the transitive pattern of a two-participant event with external argument (I) and internal argument (here).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent/initiator performing the action of coming), so it does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes manner of motion, but the internal argument 'here' functions as a locative complement specifying the destination/location of the motion event, which licenses an internal argument position.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been coming here' denotes a durative process (repeated action over three years) directed toward an implicit goal (finding figs), naturally modified by 'for three years' indicating a bounded duration with an inherent endpoint or completion state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'have been coming here' denotes a durative process unfolding over three years rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it cannot be modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have been coming here' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of repeated visits over an extended period (three years), naturally modified by 'for three years', and is homogeneous—any subinterval instantiates the same activity type.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect continuous 'have been coming here' construes the event as unfolding over a measurable duration ('for three years') rather than as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (present perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and eventive activity incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents an actual, factual situation ('I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years') without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for three years', indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0 condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for three years', indicating a continuing iterative activity rather than a completed transition to a resulting state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years' contains the duration adverbial 'for three years', but the repetition is implicitly bounded by a numerical count (three years as a discrete, completed period), and the focus is on discrete bounded visits rather than a continuous non-terminating situation holding throughout the span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' has the form 'have been' + present participle (-ing), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation across three years rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, and the duration adverbial 'for three years' makes the internal temporal span accessible.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' is in the present perfect aspect asserting a realized, habitual action in the actual world with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been coming here' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle 'coming', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been coming here' uses 'been' with the present participle 'coming' (not a past participle), and the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the action of coming, not the semantic patient; this is active voice with progressive aspect, not passive voice.

FullText: » So he told his gardener , I have been coming here looking for figs on this tree for three years . I have not found any . So cut it down ! Why should it continue using the soil ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have not found' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('any'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'found' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent) and takes a direct object 'any' (figs), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'found' licenses an internal argument ('any' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'find' denotes a punctual achievement (discovery/location of an object) that occurs at a single moment without a durative process phase; the perfect aspect 'have not found' marks the absence of this instantaneous transition into a state of discovery, not a bounded durative event with an inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of discovery/awareness; the negation 'have not found' indicates the non-occurrence of this instantaneous achievement event, which is itself an achievement-type construction.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have not found' denotes a bounded, telic situation with an inherent endpoint (the discovery of figs); it entails a specific result state and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying failure to achieve the goal, making it an accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have not found' entails a resulting state (absence of figs) and unfolds over a measurable duration ('for three years'), making it a durative activity rather than a punctual semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a change-of-state event (discovering/locating figs) with an inherent endpoint, representing dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have not found any' presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (the speaker has genuinely not found figs over three years), without any indication of counterfactuality, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wish.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('for three years'), indicating a continuing situation of searching rather than a single life experience, which triggers the set_to_0 condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('for three years'), indicating a continuing situation of non-discovery rather than a completed transition to a resulting state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have not found any' describes a discrete bounded event (the absence of finding figs) rather than a continuous situation holding over time; the focus is on the negative result of the search, not on a state or activity extending throughout the three-year period.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have not found any' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time, and the situation remains open rather than presenting a bounded completion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not found' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a past experience over three years), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have not found' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have not found' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one doing the finding) and the semantic patient 'any [figs]' as the grammatical object; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle'.

FullText: » Here is a descendant of Abraham whom Satan has kept in bonds for eighteen years . Should she be released on the Sabbath ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has kept' introduces an overt grammatical subject (Satan) and an overt direct object (a descendant of Abraham), with the prepositional phrase 'in bonds' specifying the manner of the transitive action, satisfying the condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'kept' licenses an external argument (Satan as agent) and introduces a direct object (her/a descendant), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for transitive verbs with overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has kept' licenses an internal argument ('a descendant of Abraham' functioning as the theme/patient of the keeping action), thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has kept in bonds' denotes a durative state of confinement directed toward maintaining a defined condition (bondage), naturally modified by the 'for eighteen years' adverbial indicating a bounded duration with an inherent endpoint or release, satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has kept' denotes a durative state of holding or maintaining a condition over an extended period ('for eighteen years'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the completion frame 'finish keeping', indicating an ongoing process rather than an achievement boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has kept in bonds' entails a specific result state (the state of being bound/imprisoned) and denotes a bounded situation with an inherent endpoint, rather than a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has kept in bonds' entails a resulting state (being bound/imprisoned) that persists over an extended duration (eighteen years), not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has kept' is morphologically marked as progressive/durative aspect and denotes an ongoing dynamic activity of restraint or confinement involving active maintenance, which entails eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Satan has kept in bonds' presents a situation as actually occurring in the world of the narrative (a woman has been bound for eighteen years), not as contrary to fact; it is a statement of fact within the discourse, not a counterfactual conditional or unfulfilled wish.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has kept in bonds' entails a change of state (being bound) that persists at the reference time, and the duration adverbial 'for eighteen years' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has kept in bonds' describes a change of state (transition from free to bound) where the resulting state (being in bonds) persists at the reference time, as indicated by the duration adverbial 'for eighteen years' showing the state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for eighteen years' indicating a state (being kept in bonds) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The duration adverbial 'for eighteen years' makes the internal temporal span accessible, profiling an ongoing state rather than a bounded whole, which contradicts the perfective aspect requirement.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has kept' is a present perfect that asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Satan's ongoing bondage of the woman for eighteen years), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context modifying it.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has kept' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'kept', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Satan' is the semantic agent (doer), the object 'a descendant of Abraham' is the semantic patient, and there is no passive morphology (no 'have been' + past participle); this is active voice.

FullText: » O Jerusalem , Jerusalem , you who kill the prophets ! You stone those sent to you . How often I would have gathered your children together , even as a hen gathers her own brood under her wings . But you would not have it !
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'would have gathered' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('your children'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gathered' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('your children'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'gathered' licenses an internal argument ('your children'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'gather together' denotes a durative process of collecting/assembling children toward a defined goal state (all gathered), naturally compatible with 'in X time' modification, and involves directed action toward completion of a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'gather' denotes a durative process of collecting or assembling people over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and the event can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finish gathering'), indicating it unfolds as a process phase rather than occurring at a temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have gathered' denotes a bounded, telic situation with an inherent endpoint (gathering children together is complete when they are gathered), not a dynamic durative activity without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have gathered' entails a resulting change of state (children brought together under wings) and the phrase 'How often I would have gathered' construes it as a repeated or iterative action rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'gathered' denotes a dynamic change-of-state action involving internal eventive unfolding (bringing children together), not a static condition or state that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional expressing an unfulfilled wish ('How often I would have gathered...'), where the event is understood not to have occurred, reinforced by the contrastive marker 'But you would not have it' indicating the situation is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the explicit frequency adverbial 'How often' indicating repeated or habitual instances, and the counterfactual conditional structure presents a desired repeated action in the subject's (Jesus's) life history rather than a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have gathered' expresses an unrealized, hypothetical intention rather than an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; the gathering never occurred, so no resulting state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'How often I would have gathered your children together' describes a repeated desire or intention (counterfactual) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on discrete acts of gathering, not a continuous state or habitual series characterizing the entire time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional counterfactual 'would have gathered' expresses an unrealized, hypothetical situation rather than a bounded completed event; it does not present an actual occurrence as a single bounded whole but rather a desired or intended action that did not occur.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have' in a counterfactual construction expressing a hypothetical, unrealized desire ('How often I would have gathered...'), presenting the situation as projected and non-actual rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have gathered' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which instantiates the TLTS feature according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one doing the gathering), and 'your children' is the semantic patient (the object being gathered); this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no 'been' + past participle passive morphology.

FullText: » The servant soon told his master , ‘ Your order has been carried out but there is still more room . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been carried out'), and the lexical verb 'carry out' licenses both an external argument (agent) and an internal argument (patient: 'Your order'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been carried out'), and the lexical verb 'carry out' licenses an external argument (the agent performing the action, suppressed in passive voice), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'carried out' is in passive voice with 'order' as the internal argument (theme/patient), not an external argument (agent/initiator), thus licensing an internal argument and failing the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'carry out' denotes a durative process directed toward completing a defined goal (executing an order), which naturally admits 'in X time' modification (e.g., 'the order was carried out in an hour'), satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'carry out' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state (the order has been executed), can be modified by time-point adverbials ('at noon'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot stop carrying out an order mid-execution'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been carried out' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the order was executed), not a dynamic durative situation without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'carried out' entails a transition into a resulting state (the order has been completed/executed), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has been carried out' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect and denotes a completed dynamic event (the execution of an order), involving change and eventive unfolding rather than a static state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('Your order has been carried out'), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been carried out' entails a change of state (the order is now completed) that holds at the reference time, and the resulting state is relevant for the subsequent clause ('but there is still more room'), making this non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'has been carried out' describes a change of state where an order (initially not executed) has transitioned to being executed, and this resulting state (the order is now complete/in effect) persists at the reference time, as confirmed by the context 'Your order has been carried out.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of an order) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (the completion of an order) as a single accomplished whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world within a direct speech report, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been carried out' includes 'been' followed by a progressive/passive participle 'carried out', matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been carried out' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'carry', the grammatical subject 'Your order' is the semantic patient (the thing affected by the action), and the passive voice is overtly marked.

FullText: » I will go to my father and say : ‘ Father I have sinned against God and against you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'sinned' introduces an overt internal argument ('against God and against you'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (subject 'I') and internal argument (prepositional object).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces an overt direct object 'against God and against you', satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an internal argument ('against God and against you'), which are prepositional phrases functioning as complements of the verb, thus failing the criterion for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual transgression or boundary crossing into a state of guilt/wrongdoing without a durative process phase; it cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded event, and lacks the inherent endpoint-directed process structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a durative moral or ethical state/condition rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an extended wrongdoing or violation that unfolds over time, not a boundary-crossing moment, and cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can (e.g., 'sinned at noon' is semantically odd).
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have sinned' denotes a completed action with a result state (the state of having committed sin), not a dynamic durative situation; it marks a bounded event with an inherent endpoint rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have sinned' entails a resulting change of state (transition from innocence to guilt/wrongdoing) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' denotes a change-of-state event (the transgression of committing sin) with a bounded completion point, representing dynamic eventive action rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned against God and against you' presents a situation as actually having occurred (a confession of past wrongdoing), not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have sinned' functions as a claim about the occurrence of an event in the subject's life history, focusing on whether the action has taken place rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' describes a change of moral/spiritual state (transition from non-sinned to sinned), and the resulting state of having committed sin against God and the speaker's father persists at the reference time of utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sinning) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' presents the transition event of sinning as a completed bounded whole, asserted as a discrete occurrence in the narrative sequence of the speaker's planned confession.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' appears in direct speech as a statement of realized fact ('I have sinned against God and against you'), asserting a completed action in the actual world rather than projecting, hypothesizing, or expressing desire.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have sinned' lacks the passive auxiliary 'been' and the past participle form required for VcPass; instead it is a present perfect active construction where the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one who committed the sin).

FullText: » Father , the son said : ‘ I have sinned against God and against you . I am not fit to be called your son . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have sinned' introduces an overt internal argument ('against God and against you'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (the son) and internal argument (the affected entity/patient).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent) and introduces an internal argument ('against God and against you'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sinned' licenses an internal argument ('against God and against you'), which disqualifies it from ASUnrg feature instantiation per the set_to_0_if rule.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous transgression without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary transition into a state of guilt rather than a gradual change-of-state culminating in completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a punctual transition into a culpable state (crossing a moral/ethical threshold), can be modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'I have sinned at that moment'), and represents a boundary event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (transgression against God and the father), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have sinned' construes sin as a completed action with a resulting state change (the son is now in a state of having committed sin against God and the father), not as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'sinned' denotes a change-of-state event (transgression/wrongdoing) with a defined boundary and completion, not a static condition that holds over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the son confesses a real transgression), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional structure.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned' is a negative Perfect construction (or more precisely, a positive Perfect with negative semantic content in context) that presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once, without asserting a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'sinned' describes a past action/transgression, but the clause does not entail that a resulting state (such as being in a state of sin or guilt) currently holds at the reference time; it merely reports a past occurrence without indicating a persisting consequential state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have sinned against God and against you' describes discrete bounded events (acts of sinning) rather than a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual framing indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' presents a transition event (sinning as a completed action) as a bounded whole without internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion for transition predicates asserted as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the son confesses a sin he has committed), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have sinned' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have sinned' is present perfect active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'sin'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, and the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one who committed the sin), not the patient.

FullText: » ‘ My son was dead . Now he is alive . He was lost . Now he has been found . The feasting began . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been found') and the lexical verb 'find' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, the implicit subject 'he'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' is morphologically passive with the lexical verb 'find' licensing an external argument (an implicit agent who found him), which violates the unaccusative requirement.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'found' denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the state of being found, not as an external argument/agent initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'find' denotes a punctual achievement—a near-instantaneous transition into a state of being located/discovered—without a durative process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from being lost to being found), naturally permits modification by specific time-point adverbials, and represents a boundary crossing rather than a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been found' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of finding), not a dynamic durative situation; it marks a single completed occurrence of discovery rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been found' entails a resulting change of state (transition from lost to found), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been found' denotes a change-of-state event marking the completion of the bounded process of being lost, representing dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (contrasted with the past state 'He was lost'), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been found' entails a change of state/location (from lost to found) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the parallel structure 'He was lost. Now he has been found,' indicating the son's current state is found.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been found' describes a change of state (from lost to found) where the resulting state (being found) is currently in effect at the reference time, as confirmed by the parallel structure 'He was lost. Now he has been found.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has been found' describes a discrete bounded event (the transition from being lost to being found) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or habitual series present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Now he has been found' presents a bounded transition event (finding) as a completed whole, with the passive form 'have been' + past participle asserting a discrete bounded occurrence that contrasts with the prior state 'He was lost.'
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been found' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the son is now found), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been found' includes 'been' followed by a past participle in a progressive perfect construction, which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been found' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'find', with the grammatical subject 'he' corresponding to the semantic patient (the entity that was lost and is now found), satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » Your brother returned home , the servant answered . Your father killed the prize calf because he has returned safely .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has returned' lacks an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively with only an adverbial modifier ('safely'), encoding a one-participant event without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'returned' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (he) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criteria for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'returned' denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing movement to a location, not an external argument initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'returned at noon'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at home), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and the situation is not homogeneous.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has returned' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the brother is now home, having arrived from elsewhere), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has returned' denotes a change-of-state event (arrival/coming back) involving dynamic progression from one location to another, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has returned safely' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the brother did return, the father killed the calf because of this real event), with no indication of counterfactuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has returned' entails a change of location (the brother is now home), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history experience.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'has returned' describes movement to a destination (home), but the mover has returned to the origin point, which according to the CRIs indicates experiential aspect rather than resultative (the person is back where they started, not remaining at a new destination).
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has returned safely' describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of returning home) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has returned' is a transition event (arrival/return) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has returned' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the brother's safe return is presented as a completed fact that motivates the father's action), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'We have done what we ought to have done' presents a bounded completion of an action (doing what one ought to do) as a single accomplished whole, construed as a discrete event rather than ongoing or habitual.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'ought to have done' is a modal construction expressing obligation or duty regarding a past action, not a change-of-state verb; it does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time, but rather describes what was required or expected.

FullText: He said : » Arise and go your way . Your faith has made you whole . «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made' describes a change of state (from not whole to whole), and the resulting state (being whole) is currently held at the reference time, satisfying the resultative feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has made you whole' denotes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a new state of wholeness) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial or habitual series indicating continuous extension to the reference time.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have observed' entails a resulting state of knowledge/awareness and unfolds over a measurable duration ('from my youth'), making it a durative activity rather than a punctual semelfactive occurrence.

FullText: Zacchaeus stood before Jesus and said : » Lord , I now give half of all my possessions to the poor . If I have defrauded any one I will give back four times the amount taken . «

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'defraud' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous act of deception that achieves its result state upon completion without a durative process phase, and the perfect aspect here marks a boundary transition into a state of having committed fraud rather than a measured durative accomplishment.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'If I have defrauded any one' is a conditional protasis that does not assert that a resulting state currently holds; it merely entertains the hypothetical possibility of past fraud without entailing that any fraudulent state persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'If I have defrauded any one' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of defrauding) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the conditional occurrence of a specific wrongdoing, not on a state or activity extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'If I have defrauded any one' is a conditional protasis that does not present a bounded occurrence as a discrete narrative event; instead, it expresses a hypothetical situation with an open temporal scope, making the internal development of the defrauding accessible rather than packaging it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have defrauded' appears in a conditional protasis ('If I have defrauded any one') that projects a hypothetical situation rather than asserting a realized fact, triggering irrealis marking.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'have defrauded' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the doer of the defrauding), not passive voice; passive voice requires the form 'have been + past participle' with the subject as semantic patient.

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'gained' licenses an internal argument ('what' / the profits from trading), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had gained' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of possession/accumulation, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'had gained by noon'), and represents a definable threshold crossing from not-having to having profit, meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had gained' denotes a bounded accomplishment with an inherent endpoint (a specific amount of profit/return), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and it entails a result state rather than ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had gained' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having money/profit to having it), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it is a telic accomplishment.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had gained' denotes a change-of-state event (acquiring profit/returns through trading) involving dynamic progression and a bounded outcome, not a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had gained by trading' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the servants' trading occurred before the master's return to check), not to mark a counterfactual situation; the event is presented as having actually occurred.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had gained' denotes a change of possession (acquiring money/profit) where the resulting state of increased wealth is relevant to the narrative context (the master checking on the servants' trading results), not merely whether the experience occurred in the subject's history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had gained' describes a change of possession (acquiring profit/wealth through trading), and the context indicates that the servants currently hold that gained amount at the reference time of the master's inquiry.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what they had gained by trading' describes a discrete bounded result (the profits from trading activity) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what they had gained by trading' presents a bounded completion event (the transition from not-having to having gained profit), construed as a whole accomplished fact within the narrative sequence of checking on servants' performance.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had gained' is embedded in a reported indirect question ('what they had gained') that asserts a realized fact about what the servants actually accomplished, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'had gained' is a simple past perfect active construction where the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent (doer) and the semantic patient is expressed as the direct object; it lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » The first reported : Your pound has made ten pounds more .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Your pound') and an overt direct object ('ten pounds more'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your pound as agent) and introduces a direct object (ten pounds more), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('ten pounds more'), which is the direct object/theme of the transitive verb, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'make' in this financial context denotes a punctual achievement of a result state (earning/gaining ten pounds) rather than a durative process with an inherent endpoint, and does not naturally accept 'in X time' modification in this usage.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'made' in this financial context denotes a durative process of accumulation or earning over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing characteristic of achievements.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'made' denotes a bounded accomplishment with an inherent endpoint (producing ten pounds more profit/value), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific result state and would naturally take 'in X time' rather than 'for X time'.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made (ten pounds more)' entails a resulting change of state (the pound transitioning from its original value to a new increased value), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a change-of-state event (a pound becoming ten pounds more), involving dynamic progression and an internal eventive boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('Your pound has made ten pounds more'), with no indication of counterfactual status, conditional protasis/apodosis structure, or unfulfilled wishes.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'made' entails a change of state (the pound has increased in value/quantity by ten pounds), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'made' describes a change of state in possession/value (one pound has increased to ten pounds), and the resulting state of increased value is currently held at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a transition from one pound to ten pounds) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has made' presents a transition event (the pound becoming/producing ten pounds more) as a single bounded, completed occurrence, fitting the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (your pound has produced ten pounds more as an accomplished fact), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'made', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Your pound' is the semantic agent (the entity performing the action of making/earning), and 'ten pounds more' is the semantic patient; the construction lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle), so this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » The second said : Your pound , Lord , has made five pounds .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Your pound') and an overt direct object ('five pounds'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (the pound as agent/initiator) and introduces a direct object (five pounds), violating the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has made' licenses an internal argument ('five pounds' as the direct object/theme), violating the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'make' in this context denotes a punctual transition or result state (the pound has produced/yielded five pounds) without a durative process phase; it lacks the inherent temporal unfolding toward a defined endpoint characteristic of accomplishments.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a durative process of generating or producing five pounds (profit/gain) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finished making five pounds'), indicating completion of a measurable process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a bounded accomplishment with an inherent endpoint (five pounds as the result state), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made five pounds' entails a resulting change of state (transition from one pound to five pounds), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a change-of-state event (transformation of one pound into five pounds) involving dynamic progression and internal eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the servant reports that the pound has produced five pounds), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact conditions, or unfulfilled wishes.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has made' entails a change of possession/quantity where the resulting state (five pounds now exist) holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made' describes a change of possession/increase in value (one pound becoming five pounds), and the resulting state of possession (now holding five pounds) is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the pound producing five pounds) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has made' is a transition event (change of state from one pound to five pounds) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made' is a present perfect asserting a realized accomplishment in the actual world (the pound has produced five pounds as a completed fact), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'made', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has made' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'Your pound' as the semantic agent (the entity that performs the action of making), not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: He told them , » Go into the nearby village . You will find a colt tied . No man has ever sat on him . Untie him and bring him to me .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sat' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively without an affected patient realized syntactically.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sat' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'man' who performs the sitting action), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sat' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('No man') functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in a manner of motion activity (sitting) with no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sat' denotes a punctual transition into a state (the moment of sitting down) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, and the perfect aspect here marks a state resulting from a past achievement rather than a bounded accomplishment with inherent duration.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sat' denotes a durative state or condition (remaining in a seated position) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can be naturally modified by duration adverbials (e.g., 'has sat for hours'), failing the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has sat' denotes a completed state resulting from a punctual event (the act of sitting down), not a dynamic durative activity; it marks a single occurrence without sustained engagement or homogeneous unfolding.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sat' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having sat to having sat) and is construed as a durative state rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has sat' is morphologically marked as perfect aspect denoting a completed action with present relevance; 'sat' is a change-of-state verb involving dynamic activity (the act of mounting/sitting down), which entails eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (no one has sat on the colt), without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has ever sat' with the explicit frequency adverbial 'ever' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for both negative Perfects and frequency adverbs.
DtRes_explanation: The negated clause 'No man has ever sat on him' reports that a state change (sitting on the colt) has not occurred; since the event is negated, no resulting state is entailed to hold at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sitting) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous situation holding over time; the focus is on the non-occurrence of a specific action, not on a state or activity extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' is negated with 'ever' and asserts a state of non-occurrence rather than presenting a bounded completed event; the situation remains open and is not construed as a discrete transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sat' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (no one has ever sat on the colt), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sat' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sat', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has sat' is active voice (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'sat' of an intransitive verb); the grammatical subject 'No man' is the semantic agent (the one doing the sitting), not the patient, and there is no 'been' auxiliary required for passive voice.

FullText: He was near the descent of the Mount of Olives . The entire crowd of disciples rejoiced and praised God with a loud voice for all the mighty works they had seen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'seen' (past participle of 'see') has an overt internal argument 'all the mighty works' functioning as the direct object, satisfying the criterion for a transitive construction with both external argument (they) and internal argument (object).
